chapter_info.subject: Social Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: Nazism and the Rise of Hitler

flashcards.q: What does 'genocidal' mean?
flashcards.a: Killing on a large scale leading to the destruction of large sections of people.

flashcards.q: When did Germany surrender to the Allies in World War II?
Where was the International Military Tribunal set up to prosecute Nazi war criminals?
flashcards.a: Nuremberg.

flashcards.q: How many Jews were killed during the Nazi Holocaust?
flashcards.a: 6 million.

flashcards.q: Name the three categories of crimes for which Nazi war criminals were prosecuted at Nuremberg.
flashcards.a: Crimes against Peace, War Crimes, and Crimes Against Humanity.

flashcards.q: What was the name of the democratic government established in Germany after World War I?
flashcards.a: The Weimar Republic.

flashcards.q: In which year was the Weimar Republic established?
What was the German Parliament called?
flashcards.a: Reichstag.

flashcards.q: What was the War Guilt Clause?
flashcards.a: The clause in the Treaty of Versailles that held Germany responsible for World War I and the damages suffered by the Allied countries.

flashcards.q: How much compensation was Germany forced to pay under the Treaty of Versailles?
flashcards.a: £6 billion.

flashcards.q: Which industrial area was occupied by France in 1923 when Germany refused to pay reparations?
flashcards.a: Ruhr.

flashcards.q: What is hyperinflation?
flashcards.a: A situation where prices rise phenomenally high due to excessive printing of currency, causing the value of money to collapse.

flashcards.q: What triggered the Great Depression?
flashcards.a: The Wall Street Exchange crash in 1929.

flashcards.q: What was the unemployment figure in Germany by 1932?
flashcards.a: 6 million.

flashcards.q: What is proletarianisation?
flashcards.a: Becoming impoverished to the level of the working classes.

flashcards.q: What was Article 48 of the Weimar Constitution?
flashcards.a: A provision that gave the President powers to impose emergency, suspend civil rights, and rule by decree.

flashcards.q: What was the German Workers' Party renamed as by Hitler?
flashcards.a: National Socialist German Workers' Party (Nazi Party).

flashcards.q: What percentage of votes did the Nazi Party get in 1928?
What percentage of votes did the Nazi Party get in 1932?
When was Hitler appointed Chancellor of Germany?
flashcards.a: 30 January 1933.

flashcards.q: What was the Fire Decree of 28 February 1933?
flashcards.a: A decree that indefinitely suspended civic rights like freedom of speech, press, and assembly.

flashcards.q: What did the Enabling Act of 1933 do?
flashcards.a: It gave Hitler all powers to sideline Parliament and rule by decree, establishing dictatorship in Germany.

flashcards.q: What does Gestapo stand for?
flashcards.a: Secret state police of Nazi Germany.

flashcards.q: What does SS stand for?
flashcards.a: Protection squads (Schutzstaffel).

flashcards.q: What was the name of the famous German superhighway project?
flashcards.a: Autobahn (mentioned as German superhighways).

flashcards.q: What was the name of the 'people's car' introduced by the Nazis?
flashcards.a: Volkswagen.

flashcards.q: When did Germany invade Poland?
flashcards.a: September 1939.

flashcards.q: What was the Tripartite Pact?
flashcards.a: A pact signed between Germany, Italy, and Japan in September 1940.

flashcards.q: When did Germany attack the Soviet Union?
flashcards.a: June 1941.

flashcards.q: Which battle inflicted a crushing defeat on Germany?
flashcards.a: Stalingrad.

flashcards.q: What event led to the USA entering World War II?
flashcards.a: Japan bombed the US base at Pearl Harbor.

flashcards.q: What does 'Lebensraum' mean?
flashcards.a: Living space – the concept of acquiring new territories for German settlement.

flashcards.q: Who were considered at the top of the Nazi racial hierarchy?
flashcards.a: Nordic German Aryans (blond, blue-eyed).

flashcards.q: Who were considered the 'anti-race' and arch-enemies of Aryans?
flashcards.a: Jews.

flashcards.q: What was the Euthanasia Programme?
flashcards.a: A Nazi programme under which Germans considered mentally or physically unfit were condemned to death.

flashcards.q: What were the Nuremberg Laws of 1935?
flashcards.a: Laws that defined citizenship based on racial purity; forbade marriages between Jews and Germans; stripped Jews of citizenship.

flashcards.q: What was 'The Night of Broken Glass' (Kristallnacht)?
flashcards.a: A pogrom in November 1938 when Jewish properties were vandalised, synagogues burnt, and men arrested.

flashcards.q: What was the yellow Star of David?
flashcards.a: A compulsory identity mark that Jews had to wear on their breasts from September 1941.

flashcards.q: What were the main death camps where Jews were killed?
flashcards.a: Auschwitz, Treblinka, Sobibor, Chelmno, Belzek, and Majdanek.

flashcards.q: What was the 'Final Solution'?
flashcards.a: The Nazi plan for the total elimination of Jews.

flashcards.q: What was Jungvolk?
flashcards.a: Nazi youth groups for children below 14 years of age.

flashcards.q: What was the Hitler Youth?
flashcards.a: The Nazi youth organisation for boys aged 14-18, where they were trained to worship war and hate Jews.

flashcards.q: What were Honour Crosses awarded for in Nazi Germany?
flashcards.a: To encourage women to produce many children: bronze for four, silver for six, gold for eight or more.

flashcards.q: What was the most infamous Nazi propaganda film about Jews?
flashcards.a: The Eternal Jew.

flashcards.q: What deceptive term did Nazis use for gas chambers?
flashcards.a: Disinfection-areas.

flashcards.q: Who wrote the poem about the silence of ordinary Germans?
flashcards.a: Pastor Niemoeller.

flashcards.q: What did Charlotte Beradt record in her book 'The Third Reich of Dreams'?
flashcards.a: The dreams of Jews in Nazi Germany, showing how they began believing in Nazi stereotypes about themselves.

flashcards.q: In which year was the Warsaw Ghetto archive discovered?
Who was Helmuth mentioned in the opening of the chapter?
flashcards.a: An eleven-year-old German boy whose father, a Nazi supporter, shot himself in 1945 fearing Allied revenge.

flashcards.q: What was the Dawes Plan?
flashcards.a: An American plan that reworked Germany's reparation terms to ease its financial burden after the hyperinflation crisis of 1923.

flashcards.q: What was the Spartacist League?
flashcards.a: A revolutionary group that led an uprising in Germany in 1918-1919 on the pattern of the Bolshevik Revolution.

flashcards.q: What was the Free Corps?
flashcards.a: A war veterans organisation used by the Weimar Republic to crush the Spartacist uprising.
